nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving or still water: large rivers, floodplains, swamps, lakes, and marshes with abundant aquatic vegetation; favours shallow, warm, turbid water rich in plankton and organic matter

decorPreferences: robust emergent or floating plants (water hyacinth, Amazon frogbit)
smooth large boulders or driftwood as cover

diet.notes: Highly unusual among arowanas: the African Arowana is a filter-feeder and planktivore rather than a piscivore. It possesses a specialised epibranchial organ (a spiral-shaped supra-branchial structure) that filters zooplankton, phytoplankton, algae, and fine organic particles from the water column — analogous in function to the gill rakers of filter-feeding clupeids. In aquaria, feeding is challenging: finely ground spirulina powder, liquid phytoplankton, cyclops, copepods, daphnia, very fine zooplankton suspensions, and high-quality micro-pellets (< 1 mm) can be attempted. Live or cultured rotifers and brine shrimp nauplii are often accepted. Accepts some small live invertebrates opportunistically. Avoid large meaty prey typical for other arowana species.

goodWith: large robust catfish (Synodontis spp.) in very large tanks
large cichlids of similar size if space allows
avoidWith: small fish (will be eaten or harassed)
any fish small enough to be swallowed

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Heterotis niloticus; verify current availability and license terms before use.

summary: The African Arowana (Heterotis niloticus) is a large, air-breathing bonytongue from West and Central African river systems, reaching 100 cm. Uniquely among arowanas, it is a planktivore with a specialised epibranchial filter organ — not a piscivore. Strictly public-aquarium territory; rapidly outgrows all home setups.
careNotes: The African Arowana is the sole member of the genus Heterotis and is distinct from Asian (Scleropages) and South American (Osteoglossum) arowanas in both appearance and feeding biology. It is a facultative air-breather, surfacing regularly to gulp atmospheric oxygen via a modified swim bladder; tanks must have open water surface access and a securely weighted lid, as this species is a powerful jumper. Adult size routinely reaches 90–100 cm total length, and the species is documented at up to 100 cm in the wild — making it wholly unsuitable for home aquaria at any stage beyond early juvenilhood. Juveniles are occasionally exported for the aquarium trade (typically 5–15 cm), but grow extremely rapidly — several centimetres per month under good conditions — and require 2000 L+ institutional-scale systems within 2–3 years. Water quality demands are moderate: soft to moderately hard, slightly acidic to neutral water (pH 6.5–7.5, gH 4–15) at tropical temperatures (24–30°C). The primary challenge in captivity is diet: unlike all other aquarium arowanas, H. niloticus will not reliably take standard arowana foods (large pellets, feeder fish, meaty prey). Its specialised epibranchial organ is adapted for filtering plankton; provide spirulina suspensions, live and frozen zooplankton, daphnia, copepods, rotifers, and very small (<1 mm) pellets. The species is of major importance in West African aquaculture as a fast-growing food fish and is farmed extensively in ponds across Senegal, Guinea, and elsewhere; wild populations are protected by local fisheries regulations in some range states. Not related to Asian arowanas (not subject to CITES Appendix I listing that applies to Scleropages formosus).
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is unreported in home aquaria and extremely rare in institutional settings. In the wild, pairs construct large circular nests of aquatic vegetation in shallow flooded areas; both parents guard the eggs and early fry. Fry are initially planktonic feeders. The species reaches sexual maturity at approximately 40–50 cm. Successful captive reproduction has been documented in large aquaculture pond systems in West Africa, but replication in enclosed aquaria is not considered feasible.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Heterotis niloticus
primary.notes: Primary source. Species profile covering adult size (to 100 cm), water parameters, diet (planktivore/filter-feeder, epibranchial organ), native range (West and Central Africa), temperament, tank requirements, and breeding notes. Confirmed planktivore diet type and the unusual epibranchial filter organ distinguishing this species from all other arowanas.

additional.name: FishBase — Heterotis niloticus
additional.notes: Cross-referenced taxonomy (family Arapaimidae, order Osteoglossiformes), adult size (max 100 cm TL), native range (Nile, Niger, Senegal, Gambia, Volta, Chad basins and coastal West African rivers), temperature range (22–30°C), diet category (planktivore), and aquaculture importance. Confirmed facultative air-breather and food-fish status in West Africa.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — African Arowana species profile
additional.notes: Hobbyist-oriented profile confirming the species’ unsuitability for home aquaria, filter-feeding diet, and rapid growth rate. Noted the species is occasionally traded as juveniles but reaches unmanageable size quickly. Supported the expert care level and 2000 L advisory.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — African Arowana (Heterotis niloticus)
additional.notes: Referenced for hobbyist trade perspective and diet guidance. Confirmed that the species requires a specialised plankton-based diet unlike other arowanas and is not recommended for home aquarists due to adult size.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Heterotis niloticus care and aquaculture
additional.notes: Supplementary reference covering aquaculture use in West Africa, epibranchial organ function, and captive feeding strategies. Confirmed the species’ distinction from Asian/South American arowanas and noted successful pond aquaculture in Senegal and Guinea.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding is not achieved in home aquaria. In the wild, pairs build circular vegetation nests in shallow flooded habitats and guard eggs/fry. Aquaculture-scale pond breeding documented in West Africa. Sexual maturity at approximately 40–50 cm TL.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Reaches ~100cm; food fish in West African aquaculture; needs 2000L+ tanks. Sold as small juveniles but rapidly outgrows all home setups.
